nativeRange.habitat: The Asian Bumblebee Catfish inhabits sluggish rivers and tributaries, where it conceals itself among submerged tree roots, leaf litter, and woody debris during the day and emerges to forage nocturnally. It is found in the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins as well as the Maeklong and peninsular Thai river systems.

decorPreferences: caves and hollow logs for daytime refuge
PVC pipe or ceramic tubes sized to body
tangles of driftwood roots to break sightlines
fine sand or smooth gravel substrate
subdued lighting or floating plants to dim the tank

diet.notes: In the wild, the Asian Bumblebee Catfish preys on aquatic insect larvae — including dragonfly and damselfly nymphs — small invertebrates, and occasional small fish. In captivity it readily accepts frozen or thawed meaty foods such as bloodworms, prawns, mussels, cockle, lancefish, and earthworms. Most specimens also learn to accept quality sinking pellets and carnivore wafers. Feeding after lights-out matches the species' natural foraging rhythm and tends to reduce daytime aggression toward tankmates. Mammalian or poultry meats place unnecessary strain on the fish's digestive system and should be avoided; live feeder fish carry disease risks and are best replaced with safer frozen alternatives.

goodWith: medium to large cyprinids (larger barbs, bala sharks)
avoidWith: any fish small enough to swallow (will be eaten at night)
slow-moving or long-finned fish (fin-nipping risk when underfed)
other bottom-dwelling catfish with overlapping territory

South American Bumblebee Catfish (Microglanis iheringi) — commonly confused species; SA species is far smaller (6–8 cm vs 15–18 cm), belongs to family Pseudopimelodidae, and is considerably more peaceful

summary: A boldly banded nocturnal predator from the river basins of Southeast Asia, growing to around 18 cm. Often confused in the trade with the much smaller South American Bumblebee Catfish (Microglanis iheringi), which reaches only 6–8 cm. Not suitable for community tanks with small fish.
careNotes:
The Asian Bumblebee Catfish (Pseudomystus siamensis) is a robust, secretive catfish from the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins of Southeast Asia. It is the most frequently available Pseudomystus species in the hobby and was formerly placed in the genus Leiocassis, a name still encountered in older literature and trade labels.

SPECIES CONFUSION: This species is routinely mislabelled or confused with the South American Bumblebee Catfish (Microglanis iheringi, family Pseudopimelodidae). Both share bold yellow-and-dark banding but are unrelated. Key distinctions: the Asian species grows to 15–18 cm vs the South American's 6–8 cm; the Asian species carries a pair of nasal barbels positioned just forward of the eyes running nearly vertically upward (absent in Microglanis); and the South American species displays a distinctive square dark spot on the caudal fin. Behaviourally the Asian species is a significantly more aggressive predator. Always confirm the scientific name before purchase.

TANK SETUP: A minimum 120 cm footprint tank (≈110 L) suits a single specimen. Provide multiple secure cave-like hides — ceramic tubes, sections of PVC pipe, hollow logs, or upturned pots — one per individual plus a spare, as this species is territorial at the bottom. Subdued lighting or a heavy plant canopy reduces daytime stress. Smooth substrate (fine sand preferred) protects the sensitive barbels and belly. Heaters should be shielded or positioned away from resting spots; although not fully scaleless, the skin is thin and thermal burns have been reported.

WATER QUALITY: Hardy across a wide range (pH 5.8–7.8, temperature 20–26°C, hardness 4–25°H) but sensitive to ammonia and nitrite spikes. Do not introduce into an immature tank. Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are recommended. Soft, slightly acidic water is preferred for long-term keeping and is important for any breeding attempts.

FEEDING: Strictly nocturnal feeder. Drop food after lights-out; daytime feeding is often ignored and may foul the tank. A well-fed specimen is considerably less likely to nip fins or harass tankmates.

COPPER SENSITIVITY: As a bagrid catfish, P. siamensis may be sensitive to copper-based medications. Use copper-free treatments and remove to a hospital tank when treating the main display.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reliably documented. In the wild, spawning coincides with the onset of the rainy season; juveniles appear in catches during August. Aquarium spawning attempts should provide stable, soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–6.8), a significant cool-water-then-warm temperature cycle to simulate seasonal change, and secluded spawning caves. Sexing is possible in mature adults — females are noticeably stockier than males, males possess a visible elongate genital papilla, and male light-band markings are typically more intense than in females. No captive-bred specimens are commercially available as of the research date.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudomystus siamensis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water parameters (20–26°C, pH 5.8–7.8, hardness 4–25°H), adult size (to 15 cm per page, noting the species is also traded with FishBase max of 18 cm TL), tank footprint (120 × 30 × 30 cm / 108 L for a single specimen), diet (meaty frozen and dried sinking foods), behaviour (nocturnal, territorial, predatory on small fish), native range (Mekong + Chao Phraya), sexual dimorphism, and genus history (formerly Leiocassis). Breeding noted as unreported in captivity.

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudomystus siamensis
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (18.0 cm TL male/unsexed), temperature range (20–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0), taxonomy (Siluriformes / Bagridae), native distribution (Chao Phraya, Mekong, Maeklong, peninsular Thai rivers; countries Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam), wild diet (aquatic insect larvae — dragonfly and damselfly nymphs), paternal egg-guarding behaviour, and seasonal spawning. Conservation status: Least Concern (2011).

additional.name: ScotCat Factsheet — Pseudomystus siamensis
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (16 cm), water parameters tolerances (pH 6.0–8.0, very hardy), type locality (Bangpakong River, Siam), etymology (Pseudomystus = 'False Mystus'), former genus Leiocassis, note that nocturnal feeding curbs aggression, and the substrate-spawning wild behaviour among tangled roots. Provided fin-nipping risk detail.

additional.name: FishKeeping Secrets — Asian Bumblebee Catfish
additional.notes: Trade care guide; confirmed lifespan (8–10 years), copper sensitivity note for catfish, minimum tank (55 gal / ~208 L for one fish — more conservative than SeriouslyFish; 110 L SeriouslyFish figure adopted as minimum), paternal mouthbrooding claim for breeding (conflicting with SeriouslyFish 'unreported' and ScotCat substrate-spawn data; not adopted), and temperature upper range (to 28°C noted but SeriouslyFish/FishBase 26°C used as ceiling).

additional.name: Quality Marine — Bumblebee Catfish (Microglanis iheringi)
additional.notes: Used to document the Asian/South American species confusion. Confirms South American Bumblebee Catfish (Microglanis iheringi, family Pseudopimelodidae) adult size of 3–4 inches (7–10 cm), peaceful temperament, and the shared trade name 'Bumblebee Catfish' that causes frequent misidentification at retail. The Asian species is larger, more aggressive, and from a different family and continent.

fish.breedingNotes: No confirmed captive breeding on record. Wild spawning is seasonal (onset of rainy season); males guard eggs among submerged roots. Sexing possible in adults via body profile and genital papilla. A species-specific biotope setup with soft acidic water and temperature cycling would be required for any attempt.
